params.text:
Delirium: a medical emergency

Delirium is a sudden change in a person's mental state, usually developing over hours or days. It is not the same as dementia, though the two can occur together. Crucially, delirium is a sign that something is physically wrong, and it is a medical emergency that needs urgent attention.

The key clue is sudden change from how the person normally is. Look for:

  • New or worsening confusion: far more muddled than usual, coming on quickly.
  • Changes in alertness: drowsy and hard to rouse, or restless and agitated.
  • Fluctuation: symptoms that come and go through the day, often worse later on.
  • New hallucinations or fear: seeing things or becoming suddenly frightened.

When and how to escalate

Common causes of delirium include infection (such as a urine or chest infection), dehydration, constipation, pain and medication. Many are treatable, which is exactly why fast action matters — and why you must never dismiss sudden confusion as “just their dementia”.

  • Act quickly: report a sudden change to your senior or the nurse straight away.
  • Seek medical help: the person needs urgent review — contact the GP or 111, and call 999 if they are seriously unwell.
  • Keep them safe and calm: reassure, reduce noise, and stay with them where you can.
  • Record the facts: when the change started and what you noticed helps professionals act.

Recognising delirium and escalating without delay can save a life. When in doubt, always raise your concern.

params.question: A person who is normally alert becomes suddenly and increasingly confused over a few hours, seeing things that are not there. What should you do?

answers.text: Treat it as a possible medical emergency and escalate urgently to your senior, the nurse or medical help.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Sudden confusion may be delirium, a medical emergency needing urgent escalation — never dismiss it as 'just dementia'.

answers.text: Assume it is a normal part of their dementia and monitor over the coming week.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: A sudden change is not normal and should never be assumed to be dementia. Delirium needs urgent action.

answers.text: Wait until the next scheduled GP visit to mention it.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Waiting is dangerous. Delirium is a medical emergency requiring prompt escalation, not a routine mention.

answers.text: Encourage the person to rest and see if they are better tomorrow.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Delay could be harmful. Sudden confusion must be escalated urgently for medical review.
